hso_hl.objective:
Authentication of users, services, and hardware that
interact with the entity's systems, products, services, components and
data operates through an authentication mechanism of sufficient strength
for the risk in scope, with the strictest applicable floor discharging
the most permissive on the same authentication event when both apply.
considerations: All four participating regulations impose the same substantive authentication-mechanism duty but each anchors it to a distinct OJ-text explicitness level, a distinct threshold anchor, and a distinct authentication target: GDPR silent on authentication strength — staff-side via instructions + confidentiality (Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29), data-subject-side via verification (Art. 12(6)); NIS 2 explicit MFA or continuous authentication "where appropriate" (Art. 21(2)(j) first clause); CRA permissive "appropriate control mechanisms, may include authentication" (Annex I Part I (2)(d), no explicit MFA mandate — the "may include" reading is the literal OJ anchor); DORA demanding `strong authentication mechanisms based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems` (Art. 9(4)(d) — T4 reads MFA, OJ does not say MFA; dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA but the literal OJ reading admits other `strong` mechanisms; the T4-vs-text gap is P0-flagged in `SR-DORA-002` `ambiguity_notes`).

The high-level SO describes the common authentication-mechanism duty (sufficient strength for the risk in scope, calibrated by the strictest applicable floor); the per-regulation perspective is preserved in the four sub-SOs (OJ explicitness, threshold anchor, authentication target, evidence template, recipient).

DORA is the strictest anchor and discharges the other three on a single authentication event when DORA applies on the same party; for parties not within DORA scope, NIS 2's `where appropriate` (read with the risk-based Art. 21(1) factors) is the operative authentication-strength floor.
CRA's "may include" is the product-side baseline; a CRA product implementing the NIS 2 MFA-floor or the DORA strong-authentication-floor exceeds the CRA requirement on the authentication dimension.
GDPR is the silent surrounding wrapper — neither MFA-mandating nor MFA-prohibiting, supplying instructions + confidentiality + minimisation rather than authentication-strength threshold.

The DORA T4-vs-text gap on `strong authentication` vs MFA is the operative reading-choice risk for financial entities: the dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA (R3), but a literal OJ reading admits other `strong` mechanisms (R5); the chosen reading in `SR-DORA-002` is mechanism-agnostic (R5) with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15.
hso_hl.activation: auto  # activates when ≥2 applicable regs overlap on the same authentication event

hso_per_reg.objective:
Persons authorised to process personal data act only on
documented instructions from the controller (or processor) and are
bound by confidentiality or an appropriate statutory obligation of
confidentiality (Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 + Art. 32(4) instruction-only
processing); the identity of data subjects making rights-exercise
requests is verified using reasonable means proportionate to the risk
of mis-identification (Art. 12(6) + Art. 11(2)).
considerations: GDPR is the silent-wrapper around authentication: the OJ Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 obligations govern the authorisation and confidentiality of authenticated persons (staff), and Art. 12(6) governs the verification of the data-subject requester at the rights-exercise endpoint — neither clause imposes an authentication-strength threshold.

The operational substrate is the chain-of-authority pattern (controller → processor → authorised persons, with documented instructions binding each link) and the verification pattern (data-subject → verification-on-reasonable-doubt → proportionate additional information).

A controller selecting MFA at the staff-authentication endpoint satisfies Art. 32(1)(b) confidentiality; a controller selecting single-factor password authentication is not forbidden by GDPR but must satisfy the surrounding instruction + confidentiality + minimisation obligations.
The downstream authentication failure notification cascade (Art. 33 personal-data-breach notification) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2.

hso_per_reg.objective:
Multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication
solutions are used where appropriate, and secured voice, video and text
communications as well as secured emergency communication systems are
deployed within the entity (Art. 21(2)(j)).
considerations: The OR admits both MFA and continuous-authentication as alternative authentication paradigms — they are not redundant — and the `where appropriate` hedge closes the sub-point.
ENISA's 2022 baseline security recommendations list MFA as a default for all administrative and privileged access.

The mechanism selection (FIDO2 / WebAuthn vs SMS-OTP vs TOTP vs hardware-token) is at the entity's discretion under the mechanism-agnostic R5 reading; the SMS-OTP reading is rejected for new deployments given the SS7 / SIM-swap attack surface.
NIS 2 is the most explicit OJ-text anchor among the four participating regulators (the only one with an explicit `multi-factor authentication` phrase in OJ text).
The `where appropriate` test discharges under the strictest applicable floor (DORA `strong authentication` ≈ MFA via EBA reading) when DORA applies on the same party.

The second and third clauses of Art. 21(2)(j) (secured voice/video/text + secured emergency communication systems) are addressed separately by `SR-NIS2-007` and are partially routed to D-01.2 (data-in-transit channel protection) and D-04.4 (resilience under crisis); the D-03.2 leg of NIS 2 is focused on the MFA / continuous-authentication first clause only.

hso_per_reg.objective:
Protection from unauthorised access uses appropriate
control mechanisms, which may include authentication, identity, or
access-management systems, appropriate to the identified cybersecurity
risk of the product (Annex I Part I (2)(d) — "including but not limited
to" + 3-way OR).
considerations: The OJ Annex I Part I (2)(d) text does NOT contain the phrase `multi-factor` or `continuous authentication` (the T2 mapping that imposed these phrases is rejected on the literal OJ); the OJ names authentication as one of three admitted control families (the others being identity and access-management).
CRA is the most permissive OJ-text anchor among the four participating regulators (the only one with an explicit "may include" language and no OJ-imposed authentication-strength threshold).

The CRA partial-coverage flag means that an MFA deployment at the CRA product-side exceeds the CRA requirement on the authentication dimension; a single-factor deployment at a CRA-regulated product is OJ-compliant under CRA but may not satisfy NIS 2 or DORA when those regs apply on the same party.

For an organisation across multiple regulators, the CRA leg documents the product-side authentication baseline that the manufacturer operates at; an authentication-mechanism choice that exceeds the CRA permissive baseline (e.g. selecting MFA where the risk assessment warrants) is documented in Annex VII and feeds the downstream controller / NIS 2 essential-entity / financial-entity authentication architecture.
The downstream AEV notification cascade (Art. 14) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2.

hso_per_reg.objective:
Strong authentication mechanisms (based on relevant
standards and dedicated control systems) are implemented as one of
three coordinated controls in Art. 9(4)(d), together with
cryptographic-key protection measures and risk-based data encryption.
considerations: The operational substrate is an authenticator-inventory register, AAL mappings aligned with NIST SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3 (per the EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management §145–§148 dominant supervisory expectation), and authenticator-strength calibration by risk-tier under Art. 4 proportionality.

DORA is the most demanding OJ-text anchor among the four participating regulators (the only one that names `relevant standards` and `dedicated control systems`); the OJ Art. 9(4)(d) text does NOT contain the phrase `multi-factor` — the T4-vs-text gap is P0-flagged — but the dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA. The chosen reading in `SR-DORA-002` is mechanism-agnostic (R5) with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15 that may eventually name specific authentication-strength standards.

DORA is the strictest floor; when DORA applies on the same party as the other three regulators, the DORA strong-authentication architecture discharges the NIS 2 `where appropriate` test (when NIS 2 also applies — typically only on cross-sector entities), exceeds the CRA "may include" permissive baseline, and provides the strong-authentication substrate that the GDPR silent-wrapper surrounds with instructions + confidentiality.

For pure-financial entities (NIS 2 §9 fall-back does not apply — DORA is lex specialis), DORA absorbs the NIS 2 authentication-strength obligation and the high-level SO does not aggregate across the lex-specialis boundary.
The downstream major ICT-related incident notification cascade (Art. 19) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2.

## 1. Cross-Regulation Analysis

> **Domain analysis:** [D-03.2 Multi-Factor Authentication](../../CrossRegulation/DomainAnalysis/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md)

### D-03.2 Multi-Factor Authentication

**Participants (from CRDA):** GDPR, NIS2, CRA, DORA

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ NIS2

**GDPR article (verbatim):**
> Art. 28(3)(b) requires the processor contract to ensure that persons authorised to process personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality. Art. 29 reinforces this by binding natural persons acting under the authority of controller or processor to instruction-only processing.

**NIS2 article (verbatim):**
> Article 21(2)(j) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, in its first clause, requires essential and important entities to use multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions, where appropriate.

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: staff confidentiality/instructions and data-subject verification; no explicit MFA mandate.
- NIS2 scope: MFA or continuous authentication for essential/important entities where appropriate.
- Scope overlap: Conditional when a controller is also a NIS2 entity.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: authorised-person processing and rights-request verification context.
- NIS2 trigger: continuous authentication-risk-management duty.
- Trigger alignment: PARTIAL.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: no MFA timeline; confidentiality and instruction controls are continuous.
- NIS2: MFA or continuous authentication where appropriate.
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: controller/processor accountability and data subjects indirectly.
- NIS2: entity governance and NIS competent-authority evidence.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: authorised-person instruction and confidentiality records.
- NIS2: MFA/continuous-authentication policy and risk justification.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME — COMPLEMENTARY

**Reasoning (1-2 paragraphs):** GDPR does not impose MFA. NIS2 supplies the explicit authentication mechanism, while GDPR supplies the surrounding authority and confidentiality duties. The pair is complementary because NIS2 can satisfy authentication strength while GDPR requires processing discipline for those authenticated persons.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? Conditional
- If N or Conditional: the CRDA "contradictory" classification is **scope-incorrect**; it should be "scope-disjoint" in baseline. This pair was not classified contradictory.

**Downstream implication (1-2 sentences):** Doc 07 should not claim a GDPR MFA mandate; it should use NIS2 as the MFA source and GDPR as the authorisation/confidentiality wrapper.

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ CRA

**GDPR article (verbatim):**
> Art. 28(3)(b) requires the processor contract to ensure that persons authorised to process personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality.

**CRA article (verbatim):**
> Annex I Part I (2)(d) requires protection from unauthorised access by appropriate control mechanisms, including but not limited to authentication, identity, or access-management systems; the "including but not limited to" clause keeps the floor open to other technical means while naming the three substantive control families.

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: controller/processor staff-side confidentiality and instructions.
- CRA scope: product-level unauthorised-access controls that may include authentication.
- Scope overlap: Conditional when a CRA product processes personal data or supports staff access.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: personal-data processing by authorised persons.
- CRA trigger: continuous product security design.
- Trigger alignment: DIFFERENT.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: no explicit MFA requirement.
- CRA: appropriate controls; authentication is optional within an open control family.
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: controller/processor accountability.
- CRA: manufacturer documentation and user-facing controls.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: instruction and confidentiality evidence.
- CRA: product authentication/identity/access-management evidence.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME — COMPLEMENTARY

**Reasoning (1-2 paragraphs):** The OJ-derived CRA text says authentication is included but not limited; it does not impose MFA expressly. GDPR likewise does not mandate MFA. The pair is compatible but weak as an MFA source. It should not be used to derive a hard MFA requirement without NIS2 or DORA.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? Conditional
- If N or Conditional: the CRDA "contradictory" classification is **scope-incorrect**; it should be "scope-disjoint" in baseline. This pair was not classified contradictory.

**Downstream implication (1-2 sentences):** Doc 07 should mark this pair as permissive authentication support, not an explicit MFA mandate.

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ DORA

**GDPR article (verbatim):**
> Art. 28(3)(b) requires the processor contract to ensure that persons authorised to process personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality.

**DORA article (verbatim):**
> Art. 9(4)(d) requires financial entities to implement policies and protocols for strong authentication mechanisms, based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems, and protection measures of cryptographic keys whereby data is encrypted based on results of approved data classification and ICT risk assessment processes.

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: authorised-person confidentiality and instruction-bound processing.
- DORA scope: financial-entity strong authentication and key protection.
- Scope overlap: Conditional where a financial entity processes personal data.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: personal-data processing by authorised persons.
- DORA trigger: continuous financial-entity ICT-control obligation.
- Trigger alignment: PARTIAL.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: no explicit MFA requirement.
- DORA: strong authentication based on standards and dedicated controls.
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: controller/processor accountability and supervisory authority if audited.
- DORA: financial competent authority and management body accountability.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: confidentiality undertakings and instruction records.
- DORA: strong-authentication standard, control-system and key-protection evidence.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME — COMPLEMENTARY

**Reasoning (1-2 paragraphs):** DORA provides the stronger authentication floor; GDPR provides the staff-side legal authority and confidentiality layer. The source text also preserves the DORA gap: the OJ says strong authentication, not expressly multi-factor authentication. Doc 07 should not overstate the OJ wording.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? Conditional
- If N or Conditional: the CRDA "contradictory" classification is **scope-incorrect**; it should be "scope-disjoint" in baseline. This pair was not classified contradictory.

**Downstream implication (1-2 sentences):** Doc 07 should set DORA strong authentication as the operative financial-sector floor and cite GDPR only for authorised-person controls.

#### Pair: NIS2 ↔ CRA

**NIS2 article (verbatim):**
> Article 21(2)(j) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, in its first clause, requires essential and important entities to use multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions, where appropriate.

**CRA article (verbatim):**
> Annex I Part I (2)(d) requires protection from unauthorised access by appropriate control mechanisms, including but not limited to authentication, identity, or access-management systems; the "including but not limited to" clause keeps the floor open to other technical means while naming the three substantive control families.

**Scope comparison:**
- NIS2 scope: entity-level MFA or continuous authentication.
- CRA scope: product-level appropriate access controls that may include authentication.
- Scope overlap: Conditional where a NIS2 entity deploys or manufactures CRA products.

**Trigger comparison:**
- NIS2 trigger: continuous entity authentication obligation where appropriate.
- CRA trigger: continuous product access-control obligation.
- Trigger alignment: PARTIAL.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- NIS2: MFA or continuous authentication where appropriate.
- CRA: appropriate controls, not explicit MFA.
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- NIS2: entity governance and competent-authority evidence.
- CRA: manufacturer documentation and user-facing controls.

**Content template comparison:**
- NIS2: authentication policy, factor/continuous-authentication rationale.
- CRA: product control-mechanism design and risk assessment.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME — COMPLEMENTARY

**Reasoning (1-2 paragraphs):** NIS2 is the explicit MFA/continuous-authentication source. CRA is broader and permissive, naming authentication as one possible control family. Therefore, a product can support the entity's NIS2 authentication duties, but the CRA clause alone does not impose the same MFA threshold.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? Conditional
- If N or Conditional: the CRDA "contradictory" classification is **scope-incorrect**; it should be "scope-disjoint" in baseline. This pair was not classified contradictory.

**Downstream implication (1-2 sentences):** Doc 07 should place the explicit MFA requirement under NIS2, with CRA as product-control support.

#### Pair: NIS2 ↔ DORA

**NIS2 article (verbatim):**
> Article 21(2)(j) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555, in its first clause, requires essential and important entities to use multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions, where appropriate.

**DORA article (verbatim):**
> Art. 9(4)(d) requires financial entities to implement policies and protocols for strong authentication mechanisms, based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems, and protection measures of cryptographic keys whereby data is encrypted based on results of approved data classification and ICT risk assessment processes.

**Scope comparison:**
- NIS2 scope: MFA/continuous authentication for essential/important entities.
- DORA scope: strong authentication for financial entities.
- Scope overlap: Conditional where a financial entity is also within retained NIS2 scope.

**Trigger comparison:**
- NIS2 trigger: continuous risk-based authentication duty.
- DORA trigger: continuous financial-sector authentication duty.
- Trigger alignment: PARTIAL.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- NIS2: where appropriate.
- DORA: strong authentication based on standards and dedicated controls.
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- NIS2: entity governance and NIS competent authority evidence.
- DORA: financial-entity governance and competent-authority evidence.

**Content template comparison:**
- NIS2: MFA/continuous-authentication policy, factor/justification.
- DORA: strong-authentication standard, control-system, key-protection evidence.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME — COMPLEMENTARY

**Reasoning (1-2 paragraphs):** The OJ text supports complementarity. Both regulate authentication; NIS2 is risk-anchored, DORA is strong-authentication-anchored. A financial entity also within retained NIS2 scope can satisfy both with one architectural design. There is no tension because neither clause forbids what the other mandates.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? Conditional
- If N or Conditional: the CRDA "contradictory" classification is **scope-incorrect**; it should be "scope-disjoint" in baseline. This pair was not classified contradictory.

**Downstream implication (1-2 sentences):** Doc 07 should treat the DORA strong-authentication standard as the NIS2 "where appropriate" floor in the financial-sector context.

#### Pair: CRA ↔ DORA

**CRA article (verbatim):**
> Annex I Part I (2)(d) requires protection from unauthorised access by appropriate control mechanisms, including but not limited to authentication, identity, or access-management systems; the "including but not limited to" clause keeps the floor open to other technical means while naming the three substantive control families.

**DORA article (verbatim):**
> Art. 9(4)(d) requires financial entities to implement policies and protocols for strong authentication mechanisms, based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems, and protection measures of cryptographic keys whereby data is encrypted based on results of approved data classification and ICT risk assessment processes.

**Scope comparison:**
- CRA scope: product-level appropriate control mechanisms.
- DORA scope: financial-entity strong authentication and key protection.
- Scope overlap: Conditional where a DORA financial entity deploys or manufactures a CRA product.

**Trigger comparison:**
- CRA trigger: continuous product security-design obligation.
- DORA trigger: continuous financial-entity ICT-control obligation.
- Trigger alignment: PARTIAL.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- CRA: appropriate controls; authentication is one of several admitted mechanisms.
- DORA: strong authentication based on standards and dedicated controls.
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- CRA: manufacturer documentation and product users.
- DORA: financial-entity governance and competent authority.

**Content template comparison:**
- CRA: product control-mechanism records and risk assessment.
- DORA: strong-authentication standard, control-system and key-protection evidence.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME — COMPLEMENTARY

**Reasoning (1-2 paragraphs):** CRA's product-side controls and DORA's financial-entity-side controls are layered. The DORA OJ text does not say "multi-factor" but does say "strong authentication"; CRA's Annex I (2)(d) does not require authentication specifically. A common implementation satisfies both, with DORA requiring governance depth that CRA's product-side text does not reach.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? Conditional
- If N or Conditional: the CRDA "contradictory" classification is **scope-incorrect**; it should be "scope-disjoint" in baseline. This pair was not classified contradictory.

**Downstream implication (1-2 sentences):** Doc 07 should treat CRA as the product baseline and DORA as the financial-sector deployer depth layer.

## 2. Hierarchical Security Objective

# D-03.2 Multi-Factor Authentication

> **CRDA-deep provenance.** This section was generated from
> [`../../CrossRegulation/DeepAnalysis/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md`](../../CrossRegulation/DeepAnalysis/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md)
> (6 pairs verified against OJ text — **all four participating regulations
> are GDPR, NIS 2, CRA, DORA; AI Act is out-of-scope**).

### Participants (from CRDA-deep §D-03.2)

| Regulation | Canonical SO | 1-line summary | Scope/angle |
|---|---|---|---|
| GDPR | SO-GDPR-006 (cross-ref) / SO-GDPR-005 (cross-ref) | No explicit MFA mandate; staff-side via instructions + confidentiality (Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29); data-subject-side via Art. 12(6) verification | Controller/processor; no explicit MFA; PR.AA-05 + PR.AA-06 + PR.AT-02 |
| NIS 2 | SO-NIS2-004 | MFA or continuous authentication solutions "where appropriate" + secured voice/video/text + secured emergency | Essential/important entity; Art. 21(2)(j) first clause; "where appropriate" hedge; OR of MFA and continuous authentication (not redundant); PR.AA-03 + PR.AA-05 |
| CRA | SO-CRA-016 (partial) | Protection from unauthorised access via appropriate control mechanisms, which may include authentication, identity, or access-management systems (no explicit MFA mandate) | Manufacturer; Annex I Part I (2)(d); "including but not limited to" + 3-way OR (authentication / identity / access-management); PR.AA-01 + PR.AA-05 + PR.AA-06 + DE.CM-09 |
| DORA | SO-DORA-002 | Strong authentication mechanisms (T4 reads MFA; OJ says "strong authentication") | Financial entity; Art. 9(4)(d); dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA but OJ has no "multi-factor" phrase (T4-vs-text gap); PR.AA-03 + PR.AA-04 |

### Validated relationships (from CRDA-deep §D-03.2)

All 6 pairs verified by CRDA-deep as **SAME** (CRDA-deep corrected CRDA's
initial classifications — **all 6 pairs initially marked COMPLEMENTARY**
were verified as SAME after OJ-text analysis). The underlying obligation
is the **same substantive authentication-mechanism duty** (ensuring that
the authentication event is sufficiently strong for the risk in scope);
the pairs differ on the **authentication explicitness in OJ text**, on
the **threshold anchor**, and on the **authentication target**. The four
regulators operate at four **different explicitness levels**:

- **GDPR** — no explicit MFA mandate. The OJ Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29
  obligations bind staff to instructions and confidentiality, and Art.
  12(6) governs data-subject verification at the rights-exercise
  endpoint, but neither clause imposes an authentication-strength
  threshold. GDPR supplies the **authorisation and confidentiality
  wrapper** for authentication mechanisms chosen at the controller's
  discretion; it does not name MFA.
- **NIS 2** — explicit MFA or continuous authentication "where
  appropriate" (Art. 21(2)(j) first clause). The OR admits both MFA
  and continuous-authentication as alternative authentication paradigms
  — they are not redundant — and the `where appropriate` hedge closes
  the sub-point. ENISA's 2022 baseline security recommendations list
  MFA as a default for all administrative and privileged access.
- **CRA** — "appropriate control mechanisms, which may include
  authentication, identity, or access-management systems" (Annex I Part
  I (2)(d)). The "including but not limited to" + 3-way OR
  (authentication / identity / access-management) admits MFA as one
  possible choice but does not impose it; the OJ text is **silent on
  MFA** and the CRA scope is **partial**. The T2-imposed `multi-factor
  or continuous authentication` reading is rejected because the OJ Annex
  I text does not contain these phrases.
- **DORA** — "strong authentication mechanisms" (Art. 9(4)(d)). The OJ
  text does not say "multi-factor"; the dominant EBA Guidelines
  reading on ICT and security risk management §145–§148 is MFA, but
  the literal OJ text admits other `strong` mechanisms. The T4-vs-text
  gap is **P0-flagged material reading-choice divergence** and is
  preserved in `SR-DORA-002` `ambiguity_notes`.

The authentication mechanisms are **not the same across the four
regulators in textual explicitness** but the authentication-mechanism
duty is the **same substantive obligation** — verified by CRDA-deep as
SAME after OJ-text analysis. There is no contradiction because no
clause forbids what another mandates; a single organisation across
all four regulators must operate a **layered authentication
architecture**, with the **most demanding floor (DORA's
strong-authentication)** discharging the **most permissive (CRA's
"may include")** on the same authentication event when both apply on
the same asset.

The scope-overlap column in the matrix below indicates whether the same
artefact can trigger both regulations on a single party; this is the
gating condition for the high-level SO to engage.

| | GDPR | NIS 2 | CRA | DORA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | — | Conditional (Y when controller is also NIS 2 entity; staff authentication event is the same trigger) | Conditional (Y when CRA product hosts staff- or data-subject-authentication endpoints) | Conditional (Y when DORA financial entity is also controller) |
| **NIS 2** | Conditional | — | Conditional (Y when NIS 2 entity deploys or manufactures CRA product) | Conditional (Y cross-sector entities; N pure-financial via lex specialis) |
| **CRA** | Conditional (Y integrated manufacturer-controller; product hosting the authentication endpoint) | Conditional (Y NIS 2 entity deploying CRA product) | — | Conditional (Y DORA financial entity deploying CRA product) |
| **DORA** | Conditional (Y DORA-controller) | Conditional (Y cross-sector; N pure-financial lex specialis) | Conditional (Y DORA deployer-side + CRA product-side) | — |

Legend: **Y** = scope overlap on a single artefact (high-level SO can
engage on a single party); **Conditional** = disjoint in baseline case, Y
on integrated-stack or vertical-integration scenario. CRDA-deep
scope-disjoint tests: GDPR↔NIS2 = Conditional (Y when controller is
also NIS 2 entity); GDPR↔CRA = Conditional (Y when CRA product hosts
authentication endpoints); GDPR↔DORA = Conditional (Y when DORA
financial entity is also controller); NIS2↔CRA = Conditional; NIS2↔DORA
= Conditional (Y cross-sector, N pure-financial lex specialis);
CRA↔DORA = Conditional. **All 6 pairs are Conditional**, reflecting
the layered authentication architecture pattern rather than the
identity-boundary-disjoint pattern of D-03.1.

### Cross-recipient map

D-03.2 itself does **not** generate recipient asymmetry — the
authentication-mechanism architecture is inward-looking, with the
authentication event feeding downstream authorisation (D-03.3) and
audit-trail (D-10.2) sub-domains. The recipient fragmentation across
the four regulators is at the **compliance-demonstration point**:
GDPR's supervisory authority (Art. 32 confidentiality evidence on
authentication-strength audit), NIS 2's competent authority / CSIRT
(Art. 21(2)(j) authentication-policy evidence), CRA's market
surveillance authority (Annex VII technical documentation on
authentication-mechanism choice), and DORA's national competent
authority + ESAs (Art. 9(4)(d) strong-authentication-policy evidence
under the EBA Guidelines dominant MFA reading). These are distinct
regulatory channels but they do not generate the notification cascade
of D-04.3 — they are compliance-evidence channels on the same
authentication-mechanism architecture. The D-04.3 sub-domain is the
canonical recipient-fragmentation locus for the authentication-failure
notification dimension (when an authentication failure reaches the
incident-reporting threshold).

### D-03.2.0 — High-level SecurityObjective

```yaml
id: SO-D-03.2.HL
subdomain: D-03.2
applies_to: auto  # activates when ≥2 applicable regs overlap on the same authentication event
status: static
derivation_source: ../../CrossRegulation/DeepAnalysis/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md §D-03.2
verified_relationship_basis: CRDA-deep verified (6 pairs, all SAME — CRDA initial 6 COMPLEMENTARY corrected to SAME after OJ-text analysis; 4×4 matrix complete — note: AI Act out-of-scope per AEGIS taxonomy §4; the four participating regulators target different authentication explicitness, which is preserved verbatim in the per-reg sub-SOs rather than as an unresolved tension; the DORA T4-vs-text gap on `strong authentication` vs MFA is a P0-flagged reading-choice divergence captured in `SR-DORA-002` ambiguity_notes)
emergent_tensions: 0
```

**Objective.** Authentication of users, services, and hardware that
interact with the entity's systems, products, services, components and
data operates through an authentication mechanism of sufficient strength
for the risk in scope, with the strictest applicable floor discharging
the most permissive on the same authentication event when both apply.

**Considerations.**
- All four participating regulations impose the same substantive authentication-mechanism duty but each anchors it to a distinct OJ-text explicitness level, a distinct threshold anchor, and a distinct authentication target: GDPR silent on authentication strength — staff-side via instructions + confidentiality (Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29), data-subject-side via verification (Art. 12(6)); NIS 2 explicit MFA or continuous authentication "where appropriate" (Art. 21(2)(j) first clause); CRA permissive "appropriate control mechanisms, may include authentication" (Annex I Part I (2)(d), no explicit MFA mandate — the "may include" reading is the literal OJ anchor); DORA demanding `strong authentication mechanisms based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems` (Art. 9(4)(d) — T4 reads MFA, OJ does not say MFA; dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA but the literal OJ reading admits other `strong` mechanisms; the T4-vs-text gap is P0-flagged in `SR-DORA-002` `ambiguity_notes`).
- The high-level SO describes the common authentication-mechanism duty (sufficient strength for the risk in scope, calibrated by the strictest applicable floor); the per-regulation perspective is preserved in the four sub-SOs (OJ explicitness, threshold anchor, authentication target, evidence template, recipient).
- DORA is the strictest anchor and discharges the other three on a single authentication event when DORA applies on the same party; for parties not within DORA scope, NIS 2's `where appropriate` (read with the risk-based Art. 21(1) factors) is the operative authentication-strength floor.
- CRA's "may include" is the product-side baseline; a CRA product implementing the NIS 2 MFA-floor or the DORA strong-authentication-floor exceeds the CRA requirement on the authentication dimension.
- GDPR is the silent surrounding wrapper — neither MFA-mandating nor MFA-prohibiting, supplying instructions + confidentiality + minimisation rather than authentication-strength threshold.
- The DORA T4-vs-text gap on `strong authentication` vs MFA is the operative reading-choice risk for financial entities: the dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA (R3), but a literal OJ reading admits other `strong` mechanisms (R5); the chosen reading in `SR-DORA-002` is mechanism-agnostic (R5) with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15.

### Sub-domain scope structure (verified by CRDA-deep)

The 4 participating regulations each define D-03.2 over a distinct
**authentication target** (the principal whose authentication is being
governed) and a distinct **OJ-text explicitness level**; the trigger,
the threshold anchor, the authentication target, the recipient, and
the legal anchor all depend on which scope the authentication event falls
into.

| Axis (reg) | OJ explicitness | Authentication target | Trigger | Threshold anchor | Recipient | Legal anchor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | silent | staff (authorised persons) + data subjects (rights-request endpoint) | continuous (Art. 32(1)(b) confidentiality) + triggered (Art. 12(6) verification on reasonable doubt) | (no MFA threshold; instructions + confidentiality on staff-side; reasonable-means-proportionate on requester-side) | supervisory authority (compliance-evidence audit) | Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 + Art. 12(6) |
| **NIS 2** | explicit (MFA or continuous) | staff + privileged access (entity workforce) | continuous (Art. 21(2) all-hazards) | `where appropriate` + Art. 21(1) factors (state of the art, costs, exposure, size, risk) | competent authority / CSIRT (entity governance audit) | Art. 21(2)(j) first clause |
| **CRA** | permissive (`may include authentication`) | product users + services + integrated components | continuous (operational lifetime) + risk-anchored | `appropriate control mechanisms … commensurate with identified cybersecurity risk` (Annex I Part I (2)(d)) | market surveillance authority (Annex VII technical documentation) | Annex I Part I (2)(d) |
| **DORA** | demanding (`strong authentication`) | financial-entity staff + service identities + machine identities | continuous (financial-entity operation) | `strong authentication mechanisms based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems` + Art. 6(8) appropriate level of independence | national competent authority + ESAs (EBA Guidelines dominant MFA reading) | Art. 9(4)(d) |

### Scope-overlap matrix (4×4)

(See the validated-relationships table above for the 4×4 matrix.)

### Scope-overlap principle (operational). The high-level SO
(`SO-D-03.2.HL`) engages on a given authentication event **if and only
if ≥2 applicable regulations' authentication-target scope-determiners
overlap on the same authentication event** (e.g. staff-authentication
at a controller that is also a NIS 2 essential entity operating
CRA-regulated products and running DORA-class financial ICT — a
multi-floor authentication architecture on the same staff-credential
event). Pure co-applicability of disjoint-target regulations on
different authentication events does **not** trigger cross-regulation
aggregation — see the Activation logic in the file header. For a 5-FTE
SaaS startup with GDPR + CRA applicability but no NIS 2 / DORA
applicability, only the GDPR and CRA sub-SOs activate; the high-level
SO engages on the same authentication event if both apply, with the
GDPR silent-wrapper around the CRA product-user authentication choice.
**The four authentication-target scope-determiners are layered, not
collapsed**: staff-authentication (NIS 2, DORA) on top of data-subject
verification (GDPR) on top of product-user authentication (CRA). The
high-level SO surfaces the common authentication-mechanism duty but
does **NOT** aggregate the per-reg OJ explicitness, threshold anchors,
or evidence templates. **The DORA T4-vs-text gap on `strong
authentication` vs MFA is the operative reading-choice risk** for
financial entities; the chosen reading in `SR-DORA-002` is
mechanism-agnostic (R5) with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory
reading, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15.

### Aggregation logic (from CRDA-deep verified classifications)

- 4 of 5 regulations participate (GDPR, NIS 2, CRA, DORA; AI Act is
  out-of-scope per AEGIS taxonomy §4 — no AI Act clause imposes an
  authentication-strength mandate; the AI Act threat model covers
  AI-specific attack categories handled in D-02.1, not D-03.2).
- 6 pairwise cells, verified by CRDA-deep as **all SAME** after
  OJ-text analysis: GDPR↔NIS2 (CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY),
  GDPR↔CRA (CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY), GDPR↔DORA (CORRECTED from
  COMPLEMENTARY), NIS2↔CRA (CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY),
  NIS2↔DORA (CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY), CRA↔DORA (CORRECTED from
  COMPLEMENTARY).
- The four regulations impose the **same substantive
  authentication-mechanism duty** but each anchors it to a distinct
  **OJ-text explicitness level** (GDPR silent / NIS 2 explicit /
  CRA permissive / DORA demanding), a distinct **threshold anchor**
  (none / `where appropriate` / `appropriate commensurate with risk` /
  `strong authentication based on relevant standards`), and a distinct
  **authentication target** (staff + data subjects / staff /
  product users + services + components / staff + service identities +
  machine identities). The strictest floor (DORA `strong authentication
  based on relevant standards` ≈ MFA via EBA reading) discharges the
  other three on the same authentication event when DORA applies on
  the same party; for parties not within DORA scope, the NIS 2
  `where appropriate` (read with Art. 21(1) factors) is the operative
  authentication-strength floor.
- The high-level SO describes the **COMMON OBJECTIVE** (sufficient
  authentication strength for the risk in scope, calibrated by the
  strictest applicable floor); the **per-regulation perspective**
  (OJ explicitness, threshold anchor, authentication target, evidence
  template, recipient) is preserved verbatim in the four sub-SOs.
- The high-level SO does **NOT** resolve any conditional tension —
  there are 0 conditional emergent tensions in this sub-domain;
  CRDA-deep verified all 6 pairs as SAME. Phase 1C Doc 07 does not
  need to resolve contradictions on D-03.2 because none exist on the
  authentication-mechanism duty itself; the task is to **stack the
  documentation** so that a single authentication-mechanism
  architecture (calibrated to the strictest applicable floor)
  discharges all four obligations on the same authentication event.
- **Scope caveat (CRITICAL — OJ-explicitness divergence)** — derived
  from CRDA-deep verified classifications: the high-level SO does NOT
  collapse the four OJ-text explicitness levels into a single
  authentication-strength threshold. The four regulators do not impose
  the same OJ-text wording, and the per-reg evidence template must
  anchor to the per-reg threshold. The practical operational
  convergence is on a **single authentication-mechanism architecture**
  calibrated to the **DORA strong-authentication floor** when DORA
  applies, or to the **NIS 2 `where appropriate` floor** when only NIS
  2 + CRA + GDPR apply; the CRA "may include authentication" is the
  product-side permissive baseline that any NIS 2 or DORA
  authentication-mechanism choice exceeds; the GDPR silence is the
  surrounding wrapper that requires instructions + confidentiality on
  the staff-side and verification on the data-subject-side rather
  than authentication-strength threshold. The per-reg audit trail
  remains anchored to the per-reg threshold wording even when the
  operational deployment is unified.
- **Scope caveat (CRITICAL — DORA T4-vs-text gap)** — derived from
  CRDA-deep verified classifications: the high-level SO does NOT
  resolve the DORA `strong authentication mechanisms` vs `multi-factor
  authentication` reading-choice divergence. The OJ Art. 9(4)(d) text
  contains the phrase `strong authentication mechanisms` but not
  `multi-factor`; the dominant EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk
  management §145–§148 reading is MFA, but the literal OJ reading
  admits other `strong` mechanisms (e.g. certificate-based
  authentication, hardware-token-only, PKI-bound device identity).
  The chosen reading in `SR-DORA-002` is **mechanism-agnostic (R5)
  with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading**, and the
  T4-vs-text gap is **P0-flagged** in `SR-DORA-002` `ambiguity_notes`.
  The ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15 may eventually name
  specific authentication-strength standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn,
  NIST SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3, ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.10 or successor);
  until then, the financial entity should document the chosen reading
  and the rationale.
- **Scope caveat (CRITICAL — DORA lex specialis)** — derived from
  CRDA-deep verified classifications: the NIS 2 ↔ DORA pair is **NOT
  the same** relationship on cross-sector entities as on
  pure-financial entities. For pure-financial entities (NIS 2 §9
  fall-back does not apply to entities already covered by DORA in
  scope), DORA absorbs the NIS 2 authentication-strength obligation
  via lex specialis; the high-level SO does not aggregate across the
  lex-specialis boundary in that direction. For cross-sector entities,
  the NIS 2 entity-side `MFA or continuous authentication "where
  appropriate"` obligation layers on the DORA ICT-system strong
  authentication, and the high-level SO engages on the same
  authentication event with the DORA floor as the strictest anchor.

### D-03.2.1 — Sub-SO for GDPR

```yaml
id: SO-D-03.2.GDPR
subdomain: D-03.2
applies_to: [GDPR]
inherits_from: SO-GDPR-006 (cross-ref), SO-GDPR-005 (cross-ref)
source_SR: SR-GDPR-011, SR-GDPR-012
activation: if GDPR in applicable_regs
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: SAME (CRDA-deep, D-03.2: GDPR↔NIS2 CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY; GDPR↔CRA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY; GDPR↔DORA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY)
```

**Objective.** Persons authorised to process personal data act only on
documented instructions from the controller (or processor) and are
bound by confidentiality or an appropriate statutory obligation of
confidentiality (Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 + Art. 32(4) instruction-only
processing); the identity of data subjects making rights-exercise
requests is verified using reasonable means proportionate to the risk
of mis-identification (Art. 12(6) + Art. 11(2)).

**Considerations.**
- GDPR is the silent-wrapper around authentication: the OJ Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 obligations govern the authorisation and confidentiality of authenticated persons (staff), and Art. 12(6) governs the verification of the data-subject requester at the rights-exercise endpoint — neither clause imposes an authentication-strength threshold.
- The operational substrate is the chain-of-authority pattern (controller → processor → authorised persons, with documented instructions binding each link) and the verification pattern (data-subject → verification-on-reasonable-doubt → proportionate additional information).
- A controller selecting MFA at the staff-authentication endpoint satisfies Art. 32(1)(b) confidentiality; a controller selecting single-factor password authentication is not forbidden by GDPR but must satisfy the surrounding instruction + confidentiality + minimisation obligations.
- The downstream authentication failure notification cascade (Art. 33 personal-data-breach notification) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2.

**CSF anchors (from `SR-GDPR-011` + `SR-GDPR-012` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.AA-05, PR.AA-06, PR.AT-02.
*(`SR-GDPR-011` covers the Art. 28(3)(a)/(b) + Art. 29 + Art. 32(4)
instruction-only processing anchored to PR.AA-05 (access permissions
managed per least privilege) + PR.AA-06 (access limited to authorised
users/services/hardware). `SR-GDPR-012` covers the Art. 28(3)(b)
processor-contract confidentiality anchored to PR.AA-06 + PR.AT-02
(workforce understands role-specific security responsibilities). The
two SRs together cover the authorisation + confidentiality wrapper
without imposing an authentication-strength threshold; the
authentication-strength selection is the controller's discretion,
discharged by any NIS 2 / DORA / CRA authentication-strength obligation
when those regs apply on the same party.)*

### Emergent tensions (from CRDA-deep §D-03.2)

None — sub-domain is **all-SAME in baseline (6 pairs verified as SAME
after OJ-text analysis; CRDA initial 6 COMPLEMENTARY classifications
corrected to SAME)**. No conditional emergent tensions on the
authentication-mechanism duty itself. The **OJ-explicitness divergence**
(GDPR silent / NIS 2 explicit `where appropriate` / CRA permissive
`may include` / DORA demanding `strong authentication based on
relevant standards`) is the **legitimately diverse dimension**: each
regulator names the authentication-strength threshold at a different
explicitness level, and the operational convergence is on a **layered
authentication-mechanism architecture** with the strictest applicable
floor (DORA `strong authentication`) discharging the loosest (CRA
"may include") on the same authentication event when both apply. The
**DORA T4-vs-text gap on `strong authentication` vs MFA is the
operative reading-choice risk** for financial entities — captured in
`SR-DORA-002` `ambiguity_notes` as the mechanism-agnostic R5 reading
with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading. The
**target-scope divergence** (staff-authentication / data-subject
verification / product-user authentication / service-to-service
authentication) is **nested, not disjoint**: the four authentication
targets are layered (staff-auth subsumes data-subject verification at
the rights-request endpoint; product-user authentication feeds back
into staff-auth via deployment; service-to-service auth feeds into
product-user auth via integration). The **scope-disjoint divergence**
(NIS 2 §9 fall-back / DORA lex specialis on pure-financial entities)
is **handled by the lex-specialis carve-out** — DORA absorbs the NIS 2
authentication-strength obligation and the high-level SO does not
aggregate across the lex-specialis boundary in that direction. The
high-level SO surfaces the common authentication-mechanism duty; the
per-reg OJ explicitness, threshold anchor, authentication target, and
evidence template are documented separately in the four sub-SOs and
the per-recipient evidence packs.

### Downstream implication for Phase 1C Doc 07

A company with ≥2 applicable regulations in scope of D-03.2 — with
overlapping authentication-target scope-determiners on the same
authentication event — operates a **layered authentication-mechanism
architecture** calibrated to the **strictest applicable floor**. The
high-level SO (`SO-D-03.2.HL`) describes the common
authentication-mechanism duty (sufficient strength for the risk in
scope); the per-reg perspective is preserved in the four sub-SOs (OJ
explicitness, threshold anchor, authentication target, evidence
template, recipient). **No conflict-resolution is required on D-03.2
because CRDA-deep verified all 6 pairs as SAME on the
authentication-mechanism duty.** Phase 1C Doc 07 documents the
**layered authentication-mechanism strategy**: (1) a single
authentication-mechanism architecture calibrated to the **DORA
strong-authentication floor** when DORA applies (and where the
T4-vs-text gap is resolved by the chosen R5 mechanism-agnostic reading
with R3 MFA as the dominant supervisory expectation); (2) the **NIS 2
`where appropriate`** floor as the operative authentication-strength
threshold when only NIS 2 + CRA + GDPR apply (with the risk-based Art.
21(1) factors anchoring the proportionality test); (3) the **CRA
"may include"** as the product-side permissive baseline that any NIS 2
or DORA authentication-mechanism choice exceeds; (4) the **GDPR
silent-wrapper** of instructions + confidentiality + minimisation as
the surrounding obligation that does not impose an
authentication-strength threshold but does require the
authorisation + verification architecture; (5) the **lex-specialis
carve-out** for pure-financial entities (DORA absorbs NIS 2; no
aggregation across the boundary); (6) the **DORA T4-vs-text gap** as
the operative reading-choice risk pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS
under Art. 15. For proportionality (P2), a 5-FTE SaaS with GDPR + CRA
applicability may default to the CRA `appropriate control mechanisms,
may include authentication` floor with the mechanism selection at the
manufacturer's discretion (no explicit MFA mandate; a single-factor
deployment is OJ-compliant under CRA but is not industry-state-of-
the-art per ENISA 2022 baseline); a 500-FTE DORA financial entity
must additionally document the **strong-authentication architecture
aligned with NIST SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3** per the EBA Guidelines
dominant MFA reading, with the **PKI chain-of-trust** and
**identity-assertion integrity** anchored to PR.AA-04; a DORA + NIS 2
cross-sector entity must document both the DORA strong-authentication
floor AND the NIS 2 `where appropriate` MFA / continuous-authentication
choice, with the DORA floor as the strictest anchor discharging the
NIS 2 `where appropriate` test on the same authentication event.

security_requirements.raw_md:
## 3. Security Requirements

# D-03.2 Multi-Factor Authentication — Volere requirements

> **Provenance.** Derived from `../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md`. See that file for cross-reg verified relationships (6 pairs verified as SAME — CRDA initial 6 COMPLEMENTARY corrected to SAME after OJ-text analysis), scope overlap matrix, Objective/Considerations content, OJ-explicitness divergence (GDPR silent / NIS 2 explicit `where appropriate` / CRA permissive `may include` / DORA demanding `strong authentication`), DORA T4-vs-text gap, DORA lex specialis carve-out, and aggregation logic. This file adds Volere shell fields per the `../Volere_shell.md` template.

## Participating regulations

GDPR, NIS 2, CRA, DORA (4 of 5 regs — AI Act out-of-scope per AEGIS taxonomy §4.1; no AI Act clause imposes an authentication-strength mandate; the AI Act threat model covers AI-specific attack categories handled in D-02.1, not D-03.2). CRDA-deep verified 6 pairs as all-SAME (all 6 corrected from COMPLEMENTARY after OJ-text analysis). The four regulators anchor to distinct OJ-text explicitness levels, distinct threshold anchors, and distinct authentication targets; the high-level SO surfaces the common authentication-mechanism duty while preserving the per-reg anchor and threshold — the strictest applicable floor discharges the loosest on the same authentication event when both apply.

---

## High-level requirement

```yaml
- req_id: 3.2
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: access
  title: "Authentication mechanism of sufficient strength for the risk in scope, calibrated by the strictest applicable floor (layered authentication architecture)"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-03.2.HL
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-03.2.0"

  description: |
    The product shall authenticate users, services, and hardware that interact with the entity's systems, products, services, components and data through an authentication mechanism of sufficient strength for the risk in scope, with the strictest applicable floor discharging the most permissive on the same authentication event when both apply, as the common authentication-mechanism duty converging across GDPR Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 + Art. 12(6) (silent on authentication strength — instructions + confidentiality + verification wrapper), NIS 2 Art. 21(2)(j) first clause (explicit MFA or continuous authentication `where appropriate`), CRA Annex I Part I (2)(d) (permissive `appropriate control mechanisms, may include authentication`), and DORA Art. 9(4)(d) (demanding `strong authentication mechanisms based on relevant standards`), where ≥2 of these authentication-target scope-determiners overlap on the same authentication event.

  fit_criterion: |
    A single authentication-mechanism architecture is operated, calibrated to the DORA strong-authentication floor when DORA applies on the same party (with the T4-vs-text gap resolved by the chosen R5 mechanism-agnostic reading and R3 MFA as the dominant EBA Guidelines §145–§148 supervisory expectation, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15 that may eventually name specific standards — FIDO2/WebAuthn, NIST SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3); for parties not within DORA scope, the NIS 2 `where appropriate` floor (read with Art. 21(1) factors) is the operative authentication-strength threshold; the CRA `may include` is the product-side permissive baseline that any NIS 2 or DORA authentication-mechanism choice exceeds; the GDPR silent-wrapper around authentication (instructions + confidentiality + minimisation + verification) is the surrounding obligation that does not impose an authentication-strength threshold but does require the authorisation + verification architecture; the lex-specialis carve-out discharges the NIS 2 obligation for pure-financial entities (DORA absorbs NIS 2); verified by authenticator-inventory register audit + AAL-mapping documentation review + mechanism-strength calibration records + per-reg evidence templates (DPA / national-CA / CSIRT / MSA + ESA inspection-ready evidence packs) on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: []

  nist_csf: [PR.AA-01, PR.AA-03, PR.AA-04, PR.AA-05, PR.AA-06, ID.AM-01, PR.AT-02, DE.CM-09]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [GDPR, NIS2, CRA, DORA]
    scope_overlap: conditional   # HL engages only when ≥2 applicable regs' authentication-target scope-determiners overlap on the same authentication event (see HSO scope-overlap principle)

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The four participating regulations impose the same substantive authentication-mechanism duty but each anchors it to a distinct OJ-text explicitness level, a distinct threshold anchor, and a distinct authentication target. The strictest floor (DORA `strong authentication based on relevant standards` ≈ MFA via EBA Guidelines reading) discharges the other three on the same authentication event when DORA applies on the same party; for parties not within DORA scope, the NIS 2 `where appropriate` (read with Art. 21(1) factors) is the operative authentication-strength floor; CRA's "may include" is the product-side permissive baseline; GDPR is the silent surrounding wrapper supplying instructions + confidentiality + minimisation + verification rather than authentication-strength threshold. The DORA T4-vs-text gap on `strong authentication` vs MFA is the operative reading-choice risk for financial entities — the chosen SR-DORA-002 reading is mechanism-agnostic (R5) with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15. The four authentication-target scope-determiners are layered, not collapsed: staff-authentication (NIS 2, DORA) on top of data-subject verification (GDPR) on top of product-user authentication (CRA). The HL surfaces the common authentication-mechanism duty but does NOT aggregate the per-reg OJ explicitness, threshold anchors, or evidence templates. CRDA-deep verified 0 conditional emergent tensions in this sub-domain; the task is to stack the documentation so a single authentication-mechanism architecture (calibrated to the strictest applicable floor) discharges all four obligations on the same authentication event. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-03.2.0`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

---

## Sub-requirements

### 3.2.1 — Silent wrapper (instructions + confidentiality + verification) without authentication-strength threshold

```yaml
- req_id: 3.2.1
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: access

  title: "Authentication governed by instructions + confidentiality + minimisation (no OJ authentication-strength threshold)"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-03.2.GDPR
    per_reg_so: SO-GDPR-006 (cross-ref), SO-GDPR-005 (cross-ref)
    source_sr: SR-GDPR-011, SR-GDPR-012
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-03.2.1"

  description: |
    The product shall ensure that persons authorised to process personal data act only on documented instructions from the controller (or processor) and are bound by confidentiality or an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality, and that the identity of data subjects making rights-exercise requests is verified using reasonable means proportionate to the risk of mis-identification; GDPR does not impose an authentication-strength threshold — the OJ Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 obligations govern the authorisation and confidentiality of authenticated staff, and Art. 12(6) governs the verification of the data-subject requester at the rights-exercise endpoint (GDPR Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 + Art. 32(4) + Art. 12(6) + Art. 11(2)).

  fit_criterion: |
    A chain-of-authority pattern operates (controller → processor → authorised persons, with documented instructions binding each link) and a verification pattern operates (data-subject → verification-on-reasonable-doubt → proportionate additional information); Art. 28(3)(b) processor-contract confidentiality is documented for all processors and sub-processors; Art. 5(1)(c) minimisation applies to authentication-verification artefacts; the authentication-strength selection is the controller's discretion — a controller selecting MFA at the staff-authentication endpoint satisfies Art. 32(1)(b) confidentiality, a controller selecting single-factor password authentication is not forbidden by GDPR but must satisfy the surrounding instruction + confidentiality + minimisation obligations; the GDPR silent-wrapper around authentication is the surrounding obligation that does not impose an authentication-strength threshold but does require the authorisation + verification architecture; verified by documented-instructions register audit + processor-contract confidentiality review + verification-policy inspection + minimisation register review + DPA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [3.1.1 (data-subject verification outcome feeds the rights-exercise authentication event)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AA-05, PR.AA-06, PR.AT-02]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [GDPR]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # GDPR↔NIS2 = Conditional (Y when controller is also NIS 2 entity); GDPR↔CRA = Conditional (Y when CRA product hosts staff- or data-subject-authentication endpoints); GDPR↔DORA = Conditional (Y when DORA financial entity is also controller); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    GDPR is the silent-wrapper around authentication: the OJ Art. 28(3)(b) + Art. 29 obligations govern the authorisation and confidentiality of authenticated persons (staff), and Art. 12(6) governs the verification of the data-subject requester at the rights-exercise endpoint — neither clause imposes an authentication-strength threshold. The authentication-strength selection is the controller's discretion, discharged by any NIS 2 / DORA / CRA authentication-strength obligation when those regs apply on the same party. A controller selecting MFA at the staff-authentication endpoint satisfies Art. 32(1)(b) confidentiality; a controller selecting single-factor password authentication is not forbidden by GDPR but must satisfy the surrounding instruction + confidentiality + minimisation obligations. The downstream notification cascade (Art. 33 personal-data-breach notification) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-03.2.1`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 3.2.2 — MFA or continuous authentication `where appropriate`

```yaml
- req_id: 3.2.2
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: access

  title: "Multi-factor or continuous authentication `where appropriate` (Art. 21(2)(j) first clause)"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-03.2.NIS2
    per_reg_so: SO-NIS2-004
    source_sr: SR-NIS2-006, SR-NIS2-007
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-03.2.2"

  description: |
    The product shall use multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions where appropriate, and deploy secured voice, video and text communications and secured emergency communication systems within the entity; the OR admits both MFA and continuous-authentication as alternative authentication paradigms — they are not redundant — and the `where appropriate` hedge closes the sub-point (NIS 2 Art. 21(2)(j)).

  fit_criterion: |
    An authentication-mechanism choice (MFA or continuous-authentication) is documented with risk-tier mapping anchored to Art. 21(1) factors (state of the art, costs of implementation, exposure, size, risk) and a documented `where appropriate` justification per authentication surface; ENISA 2022 baseline security recommendations (MFA as default for all administrative and privileged access) are reflected in the risk-tier mapping; the mechanism selection (FIDO2 / WebAuthn vs TOTP vs hardware-token) is at the entity's discretion under the mechanism-agnostic R5 reading; the SMS-OTP reading is rejected for new deployments given the SS7 / SIM-swap attack surface; the DORA strong-authentication floor (≈ MFA via EBA reading) discharges the `where appropriate` test when DORA applies on the same party; the second and third clauses of Art. 21(2)(j) (secured voice/video/text + secured emergency) are routed to D-01.2 (data-in-transit) and D-04.4 (resilience) and are out of scope of this D-03.2 leg; verified by risk-tier mapping review + mechanism-selection rationale records + ENISA-baseline gap assessment + competent authority / CSIRT inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [3.1.2 (workforce identity substrate feeds the staff-authentication event)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AA-03, PR.AA-05, PR.DS-02, PR.IR-03]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [NIS2]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # NIS2↔GDPR = Conditional (Y when controller is also NIS 2 entity); NIS2↔CRA = Conditional (Y when NIS 2 entity deploys or manufactures CRA product); NIS2↔DORA = Conditional (Y cross-sector entities; N pure-financial via lex specialis); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    NIS 2 is the most explicit OJ-text anchor among the four participating regulators (the only one with an explicit `multi-factor authentication` phrase in OJ text). The OR admits both MFA and continuous-authentication as alternative authentication paradigms; the `where appropriate` hedge closes the sub-point and discharges under the strictest applicable floor (DORA `strong authentication` ≈ MFA via EBA reading) when DORA applies on the same party. The second and third clauses of Art. 21(2)(j) (secured voice/video/text + secured emergency communication systems) are addressed separately by `SR-NIS2-007` and are partially routed to D-01.2 (data-in-transit channel protection) and D-04.4 (resilience under crisis); the D-03.2 leg of NIS 2 is focused on the MFA / continuous-authentication first clause only. For pure-financial entities, DORA absorbs the NIS 2 authentication-strength obligation via lex specialis; the HL does not aggregate across the lex-specialis boundary in that direction. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-03.2.2`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 3.2.3 — `Appropriate control mechanisms, may include authentication` (no explicit MFA mandate)

```yaml
- req_id: 3.2.3
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: access

  title: "Appropriate control mechanisms for protection from unauthorised access, which may include authentication"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-03.2.CRA
    per_reg_so: SO-CRA-016 (partial)
    source_sr: SR-CRA-023, SR-CRA-024
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-03.2.3"

  description: |
    The product shall protect against unauthorised access through appropriate control mechanisms, which may include authentication, identity, or access-management systems, appropriate to the identified cybersecurity risk of the product; the OJ Annex I Part I (2)(d) text does NOT contain the phrase `multi-factor` or `continuous authentication` — the T2 mapping that imposed these phrases is rejected on the literal OJ — and the OJ names authentication as one of three admitted control families (the others being identity and access-management) (CRA Annex I Part I (2)(d) — `including but not limited to` + 3-way OR).

  fit_criterion: |
    A product-side authentication-mechanism choice operates, anchored to a documented risk assessment for the product with digital elements; the OJ Annex I Part I (2)(d) text does not specify the authentication mechanism, so the SR preserves the OJ-literal language without naming MFA; the mechanism selection is at the manufacturer's discretion under the 3-way OR (authentication / identity / access-management); an MFA deployment at the CRA product-side exceeds the CRA requirement on the authentication dimension; a single-factor deployment at a CRA-regulated product is OJ-compliant under CRA but may not satisfy NIS 2 or DORA when those regs apply on the same party; reporting-on-access and attack-surface-limitation artefacts document least-privilege enforcement and authorised-only access to users/services/hardware; monitoring for attempted and successful unauthorised access (DE.CM-09) provides the visibility leg; verified by risk-assessment audit + mechanism-selection rationale records + Annex VII §2(b) + §3 + §6 + §8 technical-documentation inspection + MSA / ENISA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [3.1.3 (product-side identity substrate feeds the product-authentication mechanism choice)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AA-01, PR.AA-05, PR.AA-06, ID.AM-01, DE.CM-09]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [CRA]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # CRA↔GDPR = Conditional (Y integrated manufacturer-controller; product hosting the authentication endpoint); CRA↔NIS2 = Conditional (Y NIS 2 entity deploying CRA product); CRA↔DORA = Conditional (Y DORA financial entity deploying CRA product); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    CRA is the most permissive OJ-text anchor among the four participating regulators (the only one with an explicit `may include` language and no OJ-imposed authentication-strength threshold). The CRA partial-coverage flag means that an MFA deployment at the CRA product-side exceeds the CRA requirement on the authentication dimension; a single-factor deployment at a CRA-regulated product is OJ-compliant under CRA but may not satisfy NIS 2 or DORA when those regs apply on the same party. For an organisation across multiple regulators, the CRA leg documents the product-side authentication baseline that the manufacturer operates at; an authentication-mechanism choice that exceeds the CRA permissive baseline (e.g. selecting MFA where the risk assessment warrants) is documented in Annex VII and feeds the downstream controller / NIS 2 essential-entity / financial-entity authentication architecture. The downstream AEV notification cascade (Art. 14) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-03.2.3`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 3.2.4 — Strong authentication mechanisms (T4 reads MFA; OJ says `strong authentication`)

```yaml
- req_id: 3.2.4
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: access

  title: "Strong authentication mechanisms based on relevant standards (Art. 9(4)(d); dominant EBA MFA reading)"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-03.2.DORA
    per_reg_so: SO-DORA-002
    source_sr: SR-DORA-002
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-03_Access-Control/D-03.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-03.2.4"

  description: |
    The product shall implement strong authentication mechanisms based on relevant standards and dedicated control systems, as one of three coordinated controls in Art. 9(4)(d), together with cryptographic-key protection measures and risk-based data encryption; the OJ Art. 9(4)(d) text does NOT contain the phrase `multi-factor` — the T4-vs-text gap is P0-flagged in `SR-DORA-002` `ambiguity_notes` — but the dominant EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management §145–§148 reading is MFA (DORA Art. 9(4)(d)).

  fit_criterion: |
    An authenticator-inventory register with AAL mappings aligned with NIST SP 800-63B AAL2/AAL3 (per the EBA Guidelines §145–§148 dominant supervisory expectation) is maintained for financial-entity staff, service identities, and machine identities; the strong-authentication architecture includes FIDO2/WebAuthn for phishing-resistance, PKI for service-to-service, certificate-based authentication in service-to-service flows, signed federated-identity tokens, mutual transport authentication, and PKI chain-of-trust for identity-assertion integrity (PR.AA-04); the DORA strong-authentication floor discharges the NIS 2 `where appropriate` test (when NIS 2 also applies — typically only on cross-sector entities), exceeds the CRA `may include` permissive baseline, and provides the strong-authentication substrate that the GDPR silent-wrapper surrounds with instructions + confidentiality; for pure-financial entities, DORA absorbs the NIS 2 authentication-strength obligation via lex specialis; the T4-vs-text gap on `strong authentication` vs MFA is documented as the chosen reading (mechanism-agnostic R5 with R3 MFA as the dominant EU supervisory reading, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15); verified by authenticator-inventory register audit + AAL-mapping documentation review + PKI chain-of-trust audit logs + identity-provider assurance records + chosen-reading rationale records + national-CA + ESA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [3.1.4 (identity-assertion discipline feeds the strong-authentication event at the financial-entity boundary)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AA-03, PR.AA-04]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [DORA]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # DORA↔GDPR = Conditional (Y DORA-controller); DORA↔NIS2 = Conditional (Y cross-sector; N pure-financial lex specialis); DORA↔CRA = Conditional (Y DORA deployer-side + CRA product-side); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    DORA is the most demanding OJ-text anchor among the four participating regulators (the only one that names `relevant standards` and `dedicated control systems`); the OJ Art. 9(4)(d) text does NOT contain the phrase `multi-factor` — the T4-vs-text gap is P0-flagged — but the dominant EBA Guidelines reading is MFA. The chosen SR-DORA-002 reading is mechanism-agnostic (R5) with R3 (MFA) as the dominant EU supervisory reading, pending the ESAs Joint Committee RTS under Art. 15 that may eventually name specific authentication-strength standards. The DORA strong-authentication architecture discharges the NIS 2 `where appropriate` test on the same authentication event when NIS 2 also applies (typically only on cross-sector entities), exceeds the CRA `may include` permissive baseline, and provides the strong-authentication substrate that the GDPR silent-wrapper surrounds with instructions + confidentiality. For pure-financial entities (NIS 2 §9 fall-back does not apply — DORA is lex specialis), DORA absorbs the NIS 2 authentication-strength obligation and the HL does not aggregate across the lex-specialis boundary. The downstream major ICT-related incident notification cascade (Art. 19) is part of D-04.3, not D-03.2. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-03.2.4`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

## Decisions (CORR-029)

### Orphan SO justifications

**GDPR**: SO-D-03.2.GDPR inherits from SO-GDPR-006 and SO-GDPR-005 (cross-ref). The D-03.2 GDPR access-control obligation is preserved as a cross-reference to SOs that live primarily in D-03.1 (Identity & Authentication Management) and D-03.3 (Authorisation), per the GDPR Art. 32(1)(b) confidentiality-of-processing anchor. No standalone SO is needed in D-03.2 because the access-control dimension is operationalised through identity (D-03.1) and authorisation (D-03.3) controls; D-03.2 surfaces the cross-ref. The inherits_from field already documents this; CORR-029 formalises it as an intentional orphan (cross-ref pattern).

**CRA**: SO-D-03.2.CRA inherits from SO-CRA-016 (partial). The D-03.2 CRA access-control obligation is preserved as a partial-coverage cross-ref to SO-CRA-016 (which lives in D-03.1 — Identity & Authentication). CRA's access-control framing is anchored in Annex I Part I (2)(d) (`identify, authenticate and authorise access to data and functions') but the OJ text does not specify a distinct access-control sub-domain; the obligation is rolled into the identity-management primary in D-03.1. D-03.2 surfaces the partial cross-ref to the primary SO.
